meta.title: Claude Code Skills vs Hooks vs Agents vs Commands vs Plugins vs Rules
meta.author: Milind Nair

subtitle_lines: and when to use each one.

slides.heading: The Extension Stack
slides.subheading: Six extension points. Each solves a different problem.

items.label: Rules
items.description:
Always-on project context.
Loaded every session.

items.label: Agents
items.description:
Isolated workers with
own context windows.

items.label: Commands
items.description:
Now merged into Skills.
Slash-command triggers.

bottom_takeaway.label: Quick model
bottom_takeaway.body: Rules stay loaded, skills get called, hooks enforce, agents isolate, plugins bundle.

slides.heading: CLAUDE.md + .claude/rules/
slides.subheading: The foundation. Always-on context loaded before every conversation.

cards.title: What it does
cards.body: Defines project context, coding conventions, build commands, and constraints Claude follows every session. Every line competes for context window space, so keep it under 300 lines. Focus on what Claude would get wrong without the file.

cards.title: When to use
cards.body: Rules that apply to nearly every task: commit message formats, file access restrictions, preferred tech stack, testing commands. If you repeat the same correction to Claude more than twice, add it here.

insight.label: Key insight
insight.body: Treat CLAUDE.md like a new hire's first-day briefing: project structure, what matters, what to avoid. Teams that invest here consistently outperform teams that do not, even running the exact same model.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Put only the instructions Claude truly needs on almost every task.

slides.heading: Skills (+ Commands)
slides.subheading: On-demand context. Markdown files invoked as slash commands or loaded automatically.

cards.title: How they work
cards.body: A SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter (name, description) and markdown instructions. Lives in .claude/skills/ or ~/.claude/skills/. Claude loads the skill when its description matches the current task, or you invoke it with /skill-name.

cards.title: Key capabilities
cards.body: Supports context: fork to run in isolated subagents. Can inject dynamic context via shell commands. Supports allowed-tools restrictions and agent type selection (Explore, Plan, or custom).

cards.title: Commands merged in
cards.body: Commands have been merged into Skills. A file at .claude/commands/deploy.md and .claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md both create /deploy. Existing commands still work. Skills add subagent execution, auto-invocation, and supporting files.

inline_text.text: If you tell Claude the same thing daily, make it a skill.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Turn repeatable instructions into reusable workflows instead of reprompting.

slides.heading: Hooks
slides.subheading: Deterministic automation. Shell commands, HTTP calls, or prompts that fire at specific lifecycle events.

cards.title: Four handler types
cards.body: Command hooks run shell scripts (JSON on stdin, exit codes control flow). HTTP hooks POST to endpoints for external integrations. Prompt hooks send a prompt to Claude for semantic yes/no evaluation. Agent hooks spawn a subagent with tool access for deep verification.

cards.title: Core lifecycle events
cards.body: PreToolUse: intercept before any tool runs. PostToolUse: react after execution (format, test, log). UserPromptSubmit: validate prompts before Claude sees them. SessionStart: inject environment context. Notification: Slack or desktop alerts.

insight.label: Key difference from Skills
insight.body: A skill is a suggestion Claude follows. A hook is a rule the system enforces. Hooks are guaranteed to fire every time, with no LLM discretion involved.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Reach for hooks when the system must enforce behavior, not just recommend it.

slides.heading: Subagents
slides.subheading: Isolated workers running in their own context windows. Preserve your main session by delegating.

cards.title: How they work
cards.body: Separate Claude instances with custom system prompts, tool restrictions, and independent permissions. Defined as .md files in .claude/agents/. Built-in agents include Explore (read-only search) and Plan (research for planning). Invoke with @agent-name or let Claude auto-delegate.

cards.title: Memory + constraints
cards.body: Agents support persistent memory (MEMORY.md) scoped to project, user, or local. They can use Read, Write, Grep, and Glob. Key constraint: subagents report back to the parent but cannot talk to each other. For that, you need Agent Teams.

insight.label: Context preservation tip
insight.body: If a task touches more than about five files, isolate it in a subagent. The research happens in a separate context, and your main session gets a clean summary back.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Delegate research-heavy work so your main thread stays clean and focused.

slides.heading: Agent Teams
slides.subheading: Multiple independent Claude sessions that coordinate, message each other, and divide work in parallel.

cards.title: How they differ from Subagents
cards.body: A lead session assigns tasks. Teammates claim work from a shared task list and communicate directly. Unlike subagents, teammates collaborate and share findings without the main agent as intermediary. They can message each other and challenge assumptions.

cards.title: When to use Teams vs Subagents
cards.body: Use subagents for quick, focused workers that report back. Use Agent Teams when teammates need to share findings and coordinate collaboratively. Common pattern: planning phase to define roles, then parallel teams for execution.

insight.label: Cost note
insight.body: A 3-teammate team uses roughly 3-4x the tokens of a single sequential session. The time savings on complex tasks more than justify the cost.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Use teams only when agents need to coordinate laterally, not just report upward.

slides.heading: Plugins
slides.subheading: The distribution layer. Bundle skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers into one shareable package.

cards.title: What goes in a plugin
cards.body: A .claude-plugin/plugin.json manifest (name, description, author) plus any combination of skills, agents, hooks, and MCP configs. Install a plugin and everything merges: hooks combine, commands appear in autocomplete, skills activate automatically.

cards.title: When to reach for a plugin
cards.body: When you need to package multiple extension types for team distribution. A standalone skill handles single workflows. A plugin packages an entire domain: agents alongside skills, with hooks for quality gates and MCP servers for external integrations.

insight.label: Skills vs Plugins
insight.body: Skills are the easier entry point. You can bundle binaries, call subagents, and build real workflows from a single skill. Reach for a plugin when distributing multiple agents with hooks across a team.

bottom_takeaway.label: Best use
bottom_takeaway.body: Package the whole operating model when teammates need the same setup fast.

slides.heading: Side-by-Side
slides.subheading: How each extension point compares at a glance.

bottom_takeaway.label: Read it fast
bottom_takeaway.body: Rules are always-on, hooks are enforced, agents isolate work, plugins distribute it.

slides.heading: How They Layer Together
slides.subheading: A real-world workflow combining multiple extensions.

steps.label: CLAUDE.md
steps.description: Contains implementation standards: "write tests first", "never commit until I approve".

steps.label: Skill
steps.description: update-documentation skill activates automatically after implementations complete.

steps.label: Subagent
steps.description: Heavy implementation runs in isolated context. Main session stays focused on planning.

steps.label: Hook
steps.description: PreToolUse hook blocks commits containing .env or .pem files. Guaranteed enforcement.

steps.label: Plugin
steps.description: The entire stack packaged as a plugin. New team members install once and get everything.

slides.heading: Decision Framework
slides.subheading: Choose the right extension point for your use case.

bottom_takeaway.label: Shortcut
bottom_takeaway.body: If it is repeated, formalize it. If it must be enforced, automate it.

summary_lines: Plugins package it all for your team.
